narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time success. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 (below the typical 3.21 average), an LSAT score of 18 (well under the usual 36.70), low family income bracket, and bottom-decile rankings in both your first-year and third-year school grades—and predicted a 95% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their legal careers. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of influence, were your LSAT score, your third-year grade decile, and your first-year grade decile. Your unusually low LSAT score had the strongest pull toward failure, as it signaled to the model a major gap in the standardized skills needed for bar success, far below what most admitted students demonstrate. Similarly, landing in the lowest decile for third-year grades strongly reinforced the risk, showing the model a consistent struggle with coursework compared to peers. Even your first-year decile in the bottom rung added notable weight, highlighting early academic challenges that deviated from the stronger starts typical of bar passers. While being a full-time student aligns with the vast majority (over 92%) who pursue law this way, it couldn't offset these core academic red flags. Your situation stands out as riskier than most, with these metrics painting a picture of hurdles that have tripped up similar students before. The good news is this isn't set in stone—many overcome low starting points through targeted improvement. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your undergrad record with post-bac courses or a master's if possible, and seeking mentorship from Asian legal networks that have helped others in your position rebound. Reapplying after addressing these top concerns could shift the model's view dramatically in your favor. Keep pushing; your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer positions you well to rewrite this story.
